title: Resurgent Inflation Forces Hawkish Global Central-Bank Repricing
content: Across the four weeks inflation surprised persistently to the upside and policymakers pivoted hawkish, unwinding the prior easing narrative. Early in the window April CPI printed 3.8% YoY, above the 3.7% consensus, and long-dated USTs sold off sharply, with the 30-year briefly topping 5.19%, a near-19-year high, and the 10-year reaching roughly 4.69%. The Fed signalled no 2026 cut, pushing its first move into 2027, while the ECB delivered its first rate rise since 2023 and lifted its 2026 inflation projection to 3.0%; the BoC held with a hawkish tilt. A later US CPI at a three-year high reinforced the shift. The repricing entrenched a higher-for-longer regime across rates, firmed the USD and pressured rate-sensitive equities such as homebuilders, even as broad indices initially held near record highs.

title: Israel-Iran Conflict Drives Volatile Oil-Led Geopolitical Risk Premium
content: Middle East tensions remained the dominant swing factor through the window, injecting a fluctuating risk premium into oil and broader risk assets. Early on, improving US-Iran negotiations allowed crude to retreat, dragging the wider commodity complex lower, around 3.6% over the month, and supporting equities as they pushed to fresh records. That calm reversed sharply in mid-June when Israel struck Iran, sending oil spiking, equities lower and safe-haven gold and USTs bid. By the close of the window, ceasefire signals and reports of a possible US-Iran agreement saw crude shed close to $10 from its peak, allowing equities to recover and broaden, with the Russell 2000 outperforming. The conflict was the principal source of cross-asset volatility, fed directly into the inflation narrative through energy prices, and kept FX, commodity and equity-index direction hostage to headlines.
